results.title: Broken Link Checker for Docs Sites | DocsAlot
results.content:
# Find Every Broken Link in Your Docs

Crawl up to 10 docs pages and catch the broken links, redirect chains, timeouts, and dead ends that quietly hurt trust, SEO, and onboarding.

Try:

## What breaks trust in docs links

### 404 Not Found

Links pointing to pages that no longer exist. These create dead ends right when readers are trying to move deeper into the docs.

### Server Errors

Links returning 5xx status codes. These make the docs surface feel unstable even when the content itself is good.

### Redirects

Links that redirect to a different URL. Not always broken, but still a sign the docs graph needs cleanup.

### Timeouts

Links where the server took too long to respond. These create uncertainty for both people and crawlers.

### Connection Failures [...] ### Connection Failures

Links where the connection could not be established. These often point to expired domains, bad DNS, or stale references.

### Healthy Links

Links that return 200 OK. A healthy link graph keeps the docs surface trustworthy and easier to maintain.

Crawls up to 10 pages and checks up to 300 unique links. Export full results as CSV. Built byDocsAlot

HumanMachine

results.title: DocsAlot In Review - JustSteveKing
results.content:
03

## Developer Community

Channels, presence, and engagement infrastructure

Community is the weakest of the four pillars. The product has a Discord, a GitHub org, and social presence on X, but all three have structural issues that limit their effectiveness.

### Observations & Findings

###### Two conflicting Discord invite links across the site

Gap

The main site links to `discord.gg/Dp6EpTv4BU` while the docs footer links to `discord.gg/MPNgtSZkgK`. That fragments community and confuses new users because it is not clear whether these are separate servers or duplicate invites.

###### GitHub org is SlashML with 98 repos, mostly forks

Gap [...] ###### Sidebar typo: Creating Documenation

Gap

The sidebar heading reads `Creating Documenation`, missing the second t. For a platform whose promise is documentation quality, this is the kind of small detail that chips away at credibility because it is visible on every page.

###### llms.txt reads like marketing copy, not a machine reference

Gap

The `llms.txt` file at `docsalot.dev/llms.txt` opens with marketing language and includes sections like Why DocsAlot is the Best Choice. That cuts against the standard's purpose: it should be a factual reference for AI agents, not an SEO landing page in plain text.

Source:docsalot.dev/llms.txt

###### No concepts or architecture page explaining the data model

Opportunity [...] Gap

The quickstart is four steps: create a project, view the site, edit `index.mdx`, and publish. There is no build-something-real follow-up, no progressive path into advanced features, and the GitHub integration is buried in an optional block. A developer can finish without understanding the product.

Source:docsalot-docs.docsalot.dev/quickstart

###### Comparison pages exist, but migration guides do not

Gap

DocsAlot has a healthy set of competitor comparison pages, but zero migration guides. If someone reads DocsAlot vs GitBook and decides to switch, there is no next-step guide that shows how to move content, structure, and workflows over.

Score breakdown across education sub-dimensions:

Blog quality

Free tools

Setup Process:

`mirror-repo: the path of the repo`
`mirror-repo:slashml/magemaker` [...] That's it you're all set. Now whenever you will create a new PR, it will automatically check it, generate relevant documentation based off of the changes and in repo B that you have specified, i.e., slashml/magemaker, it will choose the specific file to edit. It will create a separate branch on top of your default branch (main or master) and then create a PR in the repo B for your review.
